title: Chapter 7: The Rumor Mill
content: Isolde didn't try to have Sera executed again.

That would have been too obvious. Too direct. The Alpha had made his position clear, and challenging him openly was a losing game. Isolde was many things — ambitious, ruthless, entitled — but she wasn't stupid.
So instead, she whispered.

It started in the noble wing. A maid overheard Lady Vane confiding in a friend. *Did you know the Moon-Marked can read minds? That's why the Alpha is so protective of her. She's probably been in his head this whole time, manipulating him.*

Then it spread to the warriors. *My cousin saw her transform. Silver fur, they said. But not natural silver. Unnatural. Wrong. The old stories say Moon-Marked wolves bring curses. That rogue attack? The timing was too convenient. What if she called it here?*
Then the servants. *They say she was planted by Aldric. A sleeper agent. Pretended to be wolf-less so no one would suspect her, and now she's got the Alpha wrapped around her finger.*

Sera heard every whisper. She'd spent her entire life attuned to the pack's gossip, learning which rumors were dangerous and which were just bored wolves entertaining themselves. These rumors were dangerous. These rumors were *weapons*.
And they were working.

Three days after the council meeting, Sera walked into the kitchen to fetch Kael's evening tea and found the room go silent. The kitchen staff — wolves she'd worked beside for months — wouldn't meet her eyes. One of the younger maids actually flinched when Sera reached for the kettle.
"The tea," Sera said quietly. "Is it ready?"
"Y-yes." The maid practically threw the tray at her. "Here. Take it. Please."
*Please.* Like Sera might curse her if she didn't move fast enough.

Sera carried the tray through the corridors, her back straight, her expression neutral. She'd learned long ago that showing weakness only made things worse. If they wanted to believe she was a dangerous Moon-Marked witch, fine. Let them believe it. Let them fear her.
It was better than pity.
*"She's sad."*

The voice in her head made her stumble. She wasn't near Kael's quarters yet — she was still two corridors away — but the link had been getting stronger. Clearer. She could hear him from farther away now, and the thoughts were more distinct.

*"Not sad. That's not the right word. Wounded? No. Defeated? No, she's never defeated. She's... lonely. That's it. She's lonely and she's trying to pretend she isn't. Just like me. Just like US. Why are we so bad at this? Why can't we just walk up to her and say 'Hey, I know the pack is being terrible to you and I want to make it better but I don't know HOW because I've spent my entire adult life being terrifying on purpose and I forgot how to be kind'?"*
Sera pressed her back against the cold stone wall and closed her eyes.

*You're not bad at this*, she wanted to say. *You're just terrified of being vulnerable. And I'm terrified of the same thing. So we're both circling each other like wounded animals, waiting for the other one to make the first move.*
But she couldn't say that. Not yet. So instead she straightened her spine, adjusted the tea tray, and walked the rest of the way to his study.

Kael was at his desk, as always. Reports. Maps. The endless bureaucracy of running a pack. He looked up when she entered, and for just a moment — just a heartbeat — his expression softened before the mask slammed back into place.
"The tea." Sera set the tray down. "Will there be anything else, Alpha?"
"Stay."
The word was simple. Direct. It hit her like a physical blow.
*"Stay. I said stay. Why did I say stay? What am I going to DO with her now that she's staying? I didn't plan this part. I didn't plan any of this. Fenris, HELP."*
*"You're on your own, buddy. You said 'stay.' Now you have to LIVE with 'stay.' I'm going to sit here and enjoy this."*

Sera stood frozen by the desk, her heart hammering. This was new. Usually Kael dismissed her immediately. Usually their interactions lasted exactly as long as it took to deliver tea and receive a terse nod.
"The rumors," Kael said. "You've heard them."
"Yes, Alpha."
"They are lies."
"I know, Alpha."
Kael set down his pen. His golden eyes fixed on her with that intensity she was beginning to recognize — the intensity that meant he was fighting a war inside his own head.

"The pack is afraid of you. That makes you dangerous. Not because you're a threat — " he added quickly, and Sera heard the echo in her head: *"She's not a threat. She's the OPPOSITE of a threat. She's a... a THREAT-DESTROYER. That's not a real word. Focus, Kael."* " — but because frightened wolves do stupid things."
"I've survived frightened wolves before."
"Not like this. Not when you're..." He paused. Searching for the word.

*"Important. Precious. Irreplaceable. No, those are all too much. Too soon. She'll think I'm crazy. CRAZIER. She'll think I'm CRAZIER than she already thinks I am. Assuming she thinks about me at all. Does she think about me?! Why would she think about me?! I've been nothing but cold and distant and occasionally wall-slamming her against bookshelves — "*
"Valuable," Kael finished. "Not when you're valuable to the pack."
*"Nailed it."*
"Valuable." Sera tested the word. "As a weapon, you mean."

"As a wolf." Kael's jaw tightened. "You transformed once. You'll transform again. When you do, you'll be one of the strongest wolves in this pack. That makes you a threat to the old order. To wolves like Isolde who believe strength comes from bloodline rather than ability."
"And what do you believe?"
The question hung in the air between them.

Kael rose from his chair. Slowly. Deliberately. The way he'd approached her that first time in the study, when he'd wall-slammed her against the bookshelf. But this time there was something different in his eyes. Something that wasn't cold at all.

"I believe," he said, his voice dropping to that low, rough register that made her stomach flip, "that strength comes from surviving. From refusing to break when the world tries to break you. From..." He stopped, his hand half-raised as if he'd been about to touch her face and caught himself.

*"FROM BEING LIKE YOU. From being brave and clever and impossibly stubborn. From transforming into a moonlight wolf in the middle of a forest because you're too angry to die. From sitting in a paralyzed Alpha's lap and staring down three traitors without flinching. THAT'S where strength comes from. Not bloodline. Not rank. YOU."*
Sera's throat was tight. "Alpha..."

"I don't know how to do this." The words came out rough, almost angry. "I don't know how to be... whatever it is you need me to be. I've spent ten years being the Wolf King. I've forgotten how to be anything else."
*"KAEL. KAEL, YOU'RE DOING IT. THIS IS CALLED 'BEING HONEST.' IT'S VERY HARD AND IT FEELS TERRIBLE BUT YOU'RE ACTUALLY DOING IT. KEEP GOING. DON'T STOP. DON'T YOU DARE STOP."*

Sera's heart was going to explode. She was certain of it. It was going to explode right here in the Alpha's study, and the last thing she'd hear would be the sound of Kael Thornwood — the terrifying, cold-blooded, throat-ripping Wolf King — confessing that he didn't know how to be human.
"You don't have to be anything," she said. "You just have to... be here. Be present. Be — "
*Mine*, her wolf whispered inside her chest. *Just be mine.*
" — honest," she finished.
Kael's hand moved. Slowly — giving her every chance to flinch away — and his fingers brushed the hair back from her left ear. His thumb traced the crescent scar.
"Magnus told me," he said quietly. "About the legend. The Moon-Marked. The prophecy."
Sera's blood went cold. "What did he tell you?"
"That you were chosen. That the Goddess linked us for a reason. That — " His thumb stilled on her birthmark. " — that you hear things. Things you shouldn't be able to hear."
Sera couldn't breathe.

*He knows. He KNOWS. He knows I can hear him. He knows I've been listening to every ridiculous, romantic, completely insane thought that's passed through his head since the moment I walked into that trial. He knows about the poetry. About the lavender. About —*
"Alpha, I — "

"I'm not angry." Kael's voice was strange. Rough and soft at the same time. "I should be. Any rational Alpha would be furious. My thoughts are... private. Strategic. The pack's safety depends on my decisions, and if someone could hear those decisions before I made them — "
"But she can't hear strategy," Sera blurted. "I mean — I can't. I only hear..." She swallowed. "I only hear *you*. The real you. The one you don't let anyone else see."
Kael went very still.
"And what," he said, his voice barely above a whisper, "do you think of the real me?"
The question was a door. Open. Waiting.
Sera could lie. She could retreat. She could protect herself the way she'd been protecting herself for twenty-two years.
Or she could be honest.

"He's..." She lifted her chin and met his golden eyes. "He's the most ridiculous, dramatic, catastrophically romantic disaster of a man I've ever encountered. He composes terrible poetry. He obsesses over the smell of lavender. He threatened to eat chocolate and die if his Alpha-self exiled me. And he is also — " her voice cracked — "the bravest, most loyal, most fiercely protective person I have ever known. He would die for this pack. He would die for *me*. And he has no idea how to say any of this out loud because he's spent ten years convincing himself that the Wolf King isn't allowed to have feelings."
She was crying. When had she started crying?

"So to answer your question, Alpha Kael Thornwood — " she wiped her eyes with the back of her hand — "I think the real you is the best man I've ever met. And I have been *terrified* that when you found out I could hear you, you would hate me for it."
The silence stretched.
And stretched.
And then Kael Thornwood, the Wolf King of the Ironmoon Pack, the coldest Alpha in three generations, did something Sera had never seen him do.
He laughed.
It was a rough, rusty sound — the laugh of someone who had forgotten how — but it was real. It was warm. It was *him*.
"Fenris is going to be insufferable about this," he said.
*"I HEARD THAT. I HEARD MY NAME. She KNOWS MY NAME. She's been listening to ME. To US. This whole TIME. EVERY POEM. EVERY MELTDOWN. EVERY 'TAIL WAGGING' COMMENT. SHE HEARD IT ALL."*
A pause.
*"...I regret nothing."*
Sera laughed too — a watery, hiccuping sound that was half relief and half hysteria. "He says he regrets nothing."

"Of course he doesn't." Kael's hand was still on her face, his thumb still tracing her birthmark. "He's been trying to get me to talk to you since the moment you walked into the trial. This is the best day of his life."

*"IT REALLY IS. THIS IS THE BEST DAY OF MY LIFE. I'M GOING TO CRY. DO WOLVES CRY? INNER WOLVES DEFINITELY CRY. I'M CRYING RIGHT NOW. THESE ARE HAPPY TEARS. TEARS OF 'SHE DOESN'T HATE US AND SHE THINKS WE'RE THE BEST MAN SHE'S EVER MET.' Someone write that down. Someone — oh wait, I'M the one who remembers things. I'LL write it down. In my brain. Forever."*
"The pack will find out eventually," Kael said. "About the link. About the prophecy. About all of it."
"I know."
"Things will get worse before they get better."
"I know."
"Are you still willing to stay?"
Sera reached up and covered his hand with hers. His skin was hot — always hot, wolf-hot — and the contact sent electricity dancing down her spine.
"I heard you tell your inner wolf you were going to marry me," she said. "After we deal with the murder-rogue situation and the traitor-wolves and the pack politics."
Kael's expression froze.

*"She heard THAT?! That was a PRIVATE thought! A very private, very sincere, very definitely-going-to-happen thought! Wait — is she bringing it up because she's HORRIFIED or because she's... because she's..."*
Sera smiled — a real smile, the kind she'd been hiding behind her hand for weeks.
"I'm holding you to that," she said.
And then, before she could lose her nerve, she rose on her toes and kissed him.
Not on the jaw this time. On the mouth.
It was brief — barely more than a brush of lips — but it was deliberate. Intentional. A promise.
When she pulled back, Kael's eyes were molten gold.
"Fenris is howling," he said hoarsely.

*"HOWLING. SCREAMING. CRYING. WRITING POETRY. ALL AT THE SAME TIME. SHE KISSED US. ON THE MOUTH. ON PURPOSE. THIS ISN'T A DRILL. THIS IS REAL LIFE. I'M GOING TO — I DON'T EVEN KNOW — I'M GOING TO RUN THROUGH THE FOREST FOR THREE HOURS AND HOWL AT THE MOON AND THEN COME BACK AND KISS HER AGAIN. Kael, you better kiss her again. YOU BETTER. I WILL NEVER FORGIVE YOU IF YOU DON'T KISS HER AGAIN."*
"He wants you to kiss me again," Sera translated.
Kael's lips curved — a real smile, small but genuine — and he pulled her closer.
"Far be it from me to disappoint my inner wolf."
The second kiss was not brief.
